krama: नमः॑ सहस्रा॒क्षाय॑ । सहस्रा॒क्षाय॑ च । च श॒तध॑न्वने । श॒तध॑न्वने च । च इति॑ च ॥

dhyana.english: May one meditate on Hara, who has three eyes, is seated on a bull, accompanied by the lord of hosts (Ganesha), and whose entire body is smeared with ashes and adorned with a crown of matted hair.
May one meditate on Hara, who has three eyes, is seated on a bull, accompanied by the lord of hosts (Ganesha), and whose entire body is smeared with ashes and adorned with a crown of matted hair.

abhinava_shankara.sanskrit:
१०२                    श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।

      अथ चतुर्थः:—— नमः कपर्दिन इति । कपर्दैः परमे-
श्वरजटाकलापः, तद्वान् कपर्दी, अनेन 'दक्षं सयञ्चं जहि
वं भटानां त्वमप्रणी हद्रजटांशको मे' इति भागवताद्प्र-
सिद्धः भगवज्जटांशवीरमद्रमहिह्मा स्मय्यैते । तेन च 'देवा
वै यञ्चरुद्रमन्तरायन् स यञ्चमविष्यत्' इत्यादिश्रुतिप्रसिद्ध-
दक्षाध्वरबन्धसमहिह्मा दशिवत: । व्युप्ताः श्रुण्डिताः केशा:
यस्य, सः व्युष्केशः, परमहंसपरिव्राजकः ; 'अथ परि-
व्राडेकशाटी शिष्यपरिवृतो मुण्डोदरपात्री' 'ज्ञानिशिक्षिन:
ज्ञानयज्ञोपवीलिन:' इत्यादिश्रुवेः ; तेन दत्तात्रेयदुर्वांसःप्र-
श्रुतयः जाबालोपनिषदादिप्रसिद्धाः, कलियुगे च 'चतुर्भिः
सिद्धतः शिष्यैः शंकरोऽवतरिष्यति' 'शंकरः सविताग्नन:'
इत्यादिपुराणप्रसिद्धपरमेश्वरावतारभावाः शंकराचार्यस्वामि-
नः, तद्रूपायेत्यर्थे । अनेन 'ब्रह्माधिपतिर्ब्रह्मणोऽधिपतिः'
इति श्रुतिप्रसिद्धं ब्रह्मविद्यासंप्रदायप्रवर्तकत्वमुक्तम् ॥

      नमः सहस्राक्षाय च शतघन्वने च ॥

      अथ पञ्चमः:——नमः सहस्राक्षायेति । सहस्रम् अनन्ता-
नि अक्षिणि यस्य, सः सहस्राक्षः, अनेन 'यः सर्वज्ञः'
इत्यादिश्रुतिप्रसिद्धः सर्वज्ञत्वमहिह्मा दशितः । शतम् अनन्ता-
नि धन्वानि धनूंषि वेद्मणवमेर्वादिरूपाणि यस्य, सः श्रत-
abhinava_shankara.english:
102                    Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam

      Now the fourth [mantra]: "Namaḥ kapardine" — Kapardas (matted hair coils) are the Lord's mass of matted locks; the one who possesses them is Kapardī. Through this name, the great glory of the Lord's matted-hair-bearing warrior Vīrabhadra — well-known from the Bhāgavata [Purāṇa] in the verse "Kill Dakṣa and his sacrifice; the hero of my matted locks shall lead the battle-forces for me" — is suggested. Thereby is indicated the glory of the destruction of Dakṣa's sacrifice, well-attested in the Śruti: "The gods were about to perform a sacrifice; Rudra would have destroyed that sacrifice" — and so forth.

      He whose hair is cut off (vyuptāḥ) and shaven (śuṇḍitāḥ) is Vyuṣkeśa (the shaven-headed one) — referring to the great Paramahaṃsa wandering ascetic (parivrājaka). This is established by the Śruti: "The wandering renunciant wears one garment, is surrounded by disciples, carries a water-pot and is shaven-headed; those who are knowers through wisdom wear the sacred thread of knowledge (jñānayajñopavītin)." Accordingly, renowned [forms of the Lord] such as Dattātreya and Durvāsas — well-known from the Jābāla Upaniṣad and similar texts — and in the Kali Age, the Śaṅkarācārya Svāmins — well-known from the Purāṇas as divine incarnations of the Supreme Lord, as stated in "Śaṅkara will descend with four accomplished disciples" and "Śaṅkara is Savitā and Agni" — are meant by this form. Through this name, the role of the Lord as the initiator of the tradition of Brahma-vidyā (knowledge of Brahman) is indicated, as well-attested in the Śruti: "He is the lord of the Brahman, the overlord of Brahman" (brahmadhipatiḥ brahmaṇo'dhipatiḥ).

      Homage to Sahasrākṣa and to Śataghānvane.

      Now the fifth [mantra]: "Namaḥ sahasrākṣāya" — He whose eyes are a thousand, i.e., infinite in number, is Sahasrākṣa (the thousand-eyed one). Through this name, the Lord's omniscience (sarvajñatva), well-attested in the Śruti by "He who is all-knowing," is indicated. He who possesses a hundred — i.e., innumerable — bows (dhanūṃṣi), such as the cosmic bow Meru and others, is Śata-
